title: Electrical Power Calculator for V, I and R
tagline: Calculate electrical power plus the missing voltage, current, or resistance using the standard power identities.
intro: Choose which two circuit values you know, enter them, and the calculator solves for power and the missing third variable instantly.

type.voltageAndCurrent_desc: Use P = V·I and R = V/I.
type.voltageAndResistance_desc: Use P = V²/R and I = V/R.
type.currentAndResistance_desc: Use P = I²R and V = IR.

error.invalid: Enter valid values for the selected input pair. Current cannot be zero in the voltage-current mode, and resistance must be positive in the voltage-resistance mode.

result.heading: Result
result.formulaVoltageCurrent: Formula: P = V·I and R = V/I
result.formulaVoltageResistance: Formula: P = V²/R and I = V/R
result.formulaCurrentResistance: Formula: P = I²R and V = IR

about.heading: About the electrical power calculator
about.body:
Electrical power is one of the core quantities in circuit analysis because it tells you how quickly electrical energy is being transferred or dissipated. In the simplest direct-current relationships, power can be written three equivalent ways: P = V·I, P = I²R, and P = V²/R. Which version you use depends on which circuit values you already know. This calculator is organized around that exact decision, letting you select voltage plus current, voltage plus resistance, or current plus resistance as your starting pair.

If you know voltage and current, the power equation is direct: multiply the two to get watts. The same pair also lets you solve resistance from R = V/I. This is common when checking a power supply, measuring a load, or estimating the equivalent resistance of a component drawing a known current from a known voltage source.

If you know voltage and resistance, the more convenient form is P = V²/R. That avoids having to calculate current separately first, although the calculator also derives current with I = V/R so you can see the full picture. This mode is useful for resistor sizing, heater calculations, and quick checks on how much current a fixed supply will push through a known resistance.

If you know current and resistance, the natural form is P = I²R and the associated voltage is V = IR. This case appears often when you know the load current and conductor or resistor value, such as in cable heating estimates, shunt calculations, or actuator drive checks. Because power scales with current squared, even modest increases in current can produce much larger heat dissipation.

These formulas assume a simple resistive relationship and steady values, which is ideal for introductory electronics, DC circuits, and many fast engineering approximations. Real AC systems, reactive components, switching waveforms, and nonlinear devices may require RMS values, power factor corrections, or more advanced analysis. Even so, the three identities used here remain the fastest and most familiar way to verify basic electrical power behavior.

examples.heading: Electrical power examples
examples.intro: These sample cases show how each input pair leads to power and the missing circuit value.

items.input: Mode: Voltage + Current; V = 12 V, I = 3 A
items.note: A 12-volt load drawing 3 amps consumes 36 watts and behaves like a 4-ohm resistance.

items.input: Mode: Voltage + Resistance; V = 24 V, R = 12 Ω
items.note: A 24-volt source across 12 ohms draws 2 amps and dissipates 48 watts.

items.input: Mode: Current + Resistance; I = 5 A, R = 8 Ω
items.note: This combination shows how quickly I²R heating grows with current.

howto.heading: How to use the electrical power calculator
steps: Choose the pair of electrical values you already know.
Enter those two values in volts, amps, or ohms as shown by the selected mode.
Click Calculate to see power in watts and the missing third circuit variable.
Use Reset to clear the form and try another circuit scenario.

items.q: Why are there three different power formulas?
items.a: They are all algebraically equivalent forms of the same circuit relationships. Depending on whether you know voltage, current, or resistance, one form is usually more convenient than the others.

items.q: When should I use P = V·I instead of P = I²R?
items.a: Use P = V·I when voltage and current are the values you already know or measure directly. Use P = I²R or P = V²/R when resistance is part of your known data and you want to avoid an extra algebra step.

items.q: Does this work for AC circuits?
items.a: It works directly for DC circuits and for resistive AC loads when you use RMS values. Reactive AC circuits with inductance or capacitance can require power factor and phase-angle analysis.

items.q: Why does the current-resistance mode show large power so quickly?
items.a: Power in that mode depends on the square of current, so doubling current makes power four times larger. That is why wire heating and resistor wattage can rise very quickly under heavier current loads.

The current-resistance mode can mathematically show zero volts and zero watts when resistance is zero, but that is an idealized short-circuit limit. In practical circuits, zero resistance assumptions are rarely realistic and often indicate a fault condition.
